text: The robotic setup includes the right robotic assist port positioned eight centimeters to the right of the camera port and the left robotic assist port positioned eight centimeters to the left. Two extra assist ports are utilized: a twelve millimeter assist port at the right iliac fossa for swabs or retractors, and the left assist port for air sail equipment to maintain pneumoperitoneum. The surgeon uses a 30-degree down camera through the camera port, curved monopolar scissors through the right assist port, and a bipolar grasper through the left robotic assist port.

The surgeon uses the left robotic bipolar grasper to stretch the rectum, revealing the angiolab fibers and hypogastric nerves. The angiolab fibers help identify the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, ensuring the preservation of key structures and nerves during dissection.
The surgeon continues the dissection down to the pelvic floor, using the left articulated wrist to apply traction and stretch the rectum, facilitating clear visualization of the dissection plane.

The surgeon uses robotic technology to demonstrate the angiolab fibers, providing a clear view of the dissection area. Traction and counter-traction are applied to follow the curve of the mesorectum as the dissection proceeds deep into the pelvis, avoiding pelvic vessels. The rectum is stretched to highlight the angiolab fibers, and instrument articulation is used to navigate challenging angles. The pelvic floor becomes visible, marking the end of the posterior dissection.

The surgeon frees the rectum from the right pelvic sidewall and carries the dissection anteriorly. The peritoneum is incised, revealing the Denonvilliers' fascia. The surgeon ensures the fat remains with the rectum, maintaining a clear fascial plane between the Denonvilliers' fascia and rectal fat. Traction and counter-traction with the left instrument help identify this plane, allowing the dissection to proceed anterolaterally, then anteriorly, and finally to the left lateral part of the rectum, while preserving the nerves.

The surgeon frees the distal sigmoid and upper rectum from the left pelvic side wall by incising the peritoneal attachments. As the peritoneum is opened, the ureter and gonadal vessels become visible. The proximal sigmoid attachments are carefully dissected, and an ethyl stitch is used to elevate the anterior pelvic peritoneal folds, keeping them clear of the dissection area.

The surgeon uses monopolar scissors to divide the peritoneal attachments, freeing the rectum from the left pelvic sidewall. The rectum is pulled to the right, and traction is applied using the left instrument to stretch the tissues, revealing the angiolyte fibers. The dissection progresses from the left lateral sidewall to anteriorly, maintaining the correct plane.

The surgeon maintains traction and counter-traction to stretch the tissues during low pelvic dissection, demonstrating the angiolab fibers. The dissection proceeds carefully to avoid damage to the rectum and surrounding structures, approaching the intersphincteric space and rectal tube. Posteriorly, the remaining fibers holding the rectum are dissected, freeing the rectum completely. A rectal examination is performed to assess the level before cross-stapling the rectum at the pelvic floor.

The surgeon uses a Covidien tri-staple purple cartridge stapler with an external articulating lever to perform a very low staple on the pelvic floor. The thicker jaw of the stapler is positioned posteriorly to avoid damaging the rectal wall. The stapler is manipulated to lie parallel to the pelvic floor and is fired twice to cross-staple the rectum at this low level.
